Event cameras respond asynchronously to pixel-wise brightness changes.
Event cameras react to the scene dynamics by generating events.
Event cameras pose the challenge of developing new methods that fully unlock their capabilities.
By 'scene dynamics', we mean the spatio-temporal changes of the visual inputs due to the relative motion of the scene w.r.t. the camera.
There are two paradigms for event processing, namely event-based and batch-based.
In both paradigms, there is the intuitive notion that more recent events should be assigned more relevancy.
Thus, each event should have a certain lifespan, based on which each one is considered active and whose contribution is considered meaningful to the computation.
Contribution is considered meaningful to the computation.

Currently, there exist three main decay strategies.
The first decay strategy is to consider the events generated within a fixed time window or by applying some constant temporal decay.
The second decay strategy considers instead a constant number of events which preserves better the data-driven nature of event cameras.
The third decay strategy explicitly models the set of active events by estimating each event’s lifetime from the corresponding velocity.
As illustrated, when the motion is fast, the accumulated events generate a blurry frame.

The proposed adaptive decay process for event cameras is presented in the context, highlighting its runtime per event and usefulness across event-based vision problems. The method removes a parameter usually tuned according to prior assumptions while introducing negligible latency.

Tab. 7 presents the runtime per event of the proposed adaptive decay process, estimating the runtime on a PC with specific hardware. The runtime per event is significantly lower than typical temporal resolution of event cameras.

The latency introduced by the adaptive decay process does not depend on the camera resolution, i.e., the number of pixels of the camera. This indicates that the method's performance is not impacted by the camera's resolution.

By considering the proposed adaptive decay process, the performances of baseline methods are consistently improved. This enhancement is seen across several event-based vision problems, leading to better results.

The proposed method removes the need for parameter tuning based on prior assumptions, leading to improved performance and introducing negligible latency. This feature makes the method more efficient and user-friendly in event-based applications.

Future research avenues include additional empirical evaluation regarding the weight threshold value and exploring other forms of temporally varying decay rates in the context of the proposed adaptive decay process for event cameras.
The evaluation involves considering the weight threshold value and other forms of temporally varying decay rates.
The proposed method assumes events come from a single motion.
The proposed method could fail in scenarios where objects are independently moving.
